titleStmt.title: "Title of Work"
Glosses
titleStmt.funder: American Philosophical Society, Beinecke Library, Bibliographical Society of America, Blossman Foundation, Huntington Library, Louisiana Board of Regents, National Endowment for the Humanities, NINES, Pierpont Morgan Library, South Central Modern Language Association, and Southeastern Louisiana University

distributor.name: Southeastern Louisiana University Department of English

sourceDesc.p: Born digitally, prepared by David C. Hanson.

div.note: The Ruskins (that being, his mother and father) were said to have been church-goers, but not exactly involved in the greater faith community at large. As such, young Ruskin’s view on religion and fath was likely influenced by sermons (of which he wrote extensively in his young diary, critiquing their quality). The language he employs throughout this poem mirrors that of Evangelical sermons, the basis of young Ruskin’s faith (and likely content his mother approved of, despite her distaste of violent imagery.) Ruskin would have been roughly eleven years old at the time of writing, and, given his mother’s penchant for reading and proofreading his work, it can be inferred that the wording and content of this poem may be influenced by that fact.

“billow” in this context may be used for its conventional use, as a swelling of wind. However, as the Oxford English Dictionary states, the word also held a figurative meaning in Ruskin’s time, “death as an overwhelming flood”, harkening back to the overall darker thematic element of this poem.

Later in life, in one of his letters to a friend in college, he would reminisce about his mother’s fear-based teachings on religious theology, citing eternal burning hell and damnation of the soul as a focal point of her teachings. Again, a lot of the language within this poem is likely fueled by his fascination with religious sermons and his mother’s strict teachings on the matter. A lot of his later questioning on faith would be based on the concept of eternality.
References the harkening of the end of times as prophesied in Revelations.
The idea, prevalent in Christianity, that the blood and body of Christ can save those who hold faith.
